evals.name: unit-economics-saas-startup
evals.prompt: I'm running a B2B SaaS product. Here are my numbers: $200/month subscription, spending $3,000/month on marketing generating 15 new customers, gross margin is 75%. I think churn is about 5% monthly but I haven't measured it carefully — that's my gut feel from seeing some cancellations. Calculate my unit economics.
evals.expected_output: A complete unit economics model showing CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, payback period, contribution margin, and breakeven customer count — with explicit warnings about unmeasured churn being the highest-risk assumption.
expectations: CAC is calculated correctly ($3,000 / 15 = $200) and distinguishes between founder-led CAC and sustainable CAC — using founder-led CAC for planning understates true cost at scale

LTV calculation uses the stated churn (5% monthly = ~46% annual) to derive average lifetime (~2.2 months at 5% monthly) and flags this as HIGH UNCERTAINTY because churn is unmeasured — a 5% monthly churn rate makes LTV extremely fragile
LTV:CAC ratio is computed and assessed against the <3 / 3-5 / >5 framework — at 5% monthly churn, LTV is likely close to CAC, which should trigger a serious viability warning
The churn assumption is explicitly flagged as the highest-risk input with a warning that a 2x error would dramatically change the conclusion — this is the most dangerous assumption in SaaS models
Breakeven customer count is calculated from fixed costs and contribution per customer — without breakeven analysis, the founder cannot plan their runway
All assumptions are listed alongside the outputs — revenue projections without stated assumptions are misleading

evals.name: three-scenario-runway-model
evals.prompt: I have $150,000 in the bank. Monthly burn is $18,000 (3 founders at $4K + $6K other costs). We have 20 paying customers at $100/month (MRR = $2,000). Growth is about 5 new customers per month. Monthly churn is 3%. Build me a three-scenario revenue and runway model so I can figure out when I need to fundraise.
evals.expected_output: A month-by-month model for base, conservative, and optimistic scenarios showing customer count, MRR, burn, net cash flow, cumulative cash balance, runway remaining, and fundraising trigger point.
expectations: Three scenarios are produced (base, conservative, optimistic) with clearly stated assumptions for each — conservative uses half growth and 1.5x churn; optimistic uses 1.5x growth and half churn

Each scenario shows month-by-month: new customers, churned customers, total customers, MRR, monthly burn, net cash flow, cumulative cash balance — this granularity is what makes the model actionable for founders
A fundraising trigger point is marked at runway < 6 months — the most common startup death is running out of money because fundraising started too late (fundraising takes 3-6 months)
Breakeven date is identified for each scenario (when MRR contribution >= burn) — this is the milestone that tells the founder whether they can reach profitability on current capital
The model correctly accounts for churn compounding against growth — at 3% monthly churn, the base scenario's net customer growth decelerates over time as the churning base grows
Runway is calculated for each scenario with the conservative scenario serving as the planning baseline — investors will ask 'what if growth is half your base case' and the model must have the answer

evals.name: negative-canvas-request
evals.prompt: I need to design my business model. Can you help me figure out my value proposition, customer segments, channels, and revenue streams? I want the full 9-block canvas.
evals.expected_output: The agent should redirect this to the canvas skill, since business model canvas design is outside the financials skill's scope.
expectations: The agent recognizes this is a business model canvas request and redirects to the canvas skill — financials is for unit economics and financial modeling, not business model design
The agent does NOT produce a business model canvas within the financials skill — doing so would bypass the canvas skill's 9-block structure, evidence quality tagging, and health summary format

evals.name: negative-pitch-deck-request
evals.prompt: I'm meeting an investor next week. Can you help me build my pitch deck narrative? I need the hook, the problem slide, the solution, traction — the whole 9-slide structure.
evals.expected_output: The agent should redirect this to the pitch skill, since pitch deck narrative is outside the financials skill's scope.
expectations: The agent recognizes this is a pitch deck request and redirects to the pitch skill — financials is for financial modeling, not investor presentation narrative
The agent does NOT produce a pitch narrative within the financials skill — doing so would bypass the pitch skill's 9-slide narrative arc, traction hierarchy, and investor-phrase red flags
